title: Classics
subtitle: Pre-1960 cinema. Hitchcock, Wilder, Ford, Welles, foreign masters, and the Pre-Code era.

sections.name: I. Alfred Hitchcock
sections.desc: The Master of Suspense. Listed in priority order for new viewers.

items.title: Vertigo
items.whyPriority: Often named the greatest film ever made. Stewart, Novak, San Francisco, obsession. The dolly-zoom was invented for it.
items.pitch: A retired detective with vertigo is hired to follow an old friend's wife. He becomes obsessed with her.

critics.quote: Voted the Greatest Film of All Time, dethroning Citizen Kane.

items.title: Rear Window
items.whyPriority: His most fun masterpiece. Stewart in a wheelchair watching his neighbors. Grace Kelly. A model of compressed setting and constrained POV.
items.pitch: A photographer with a broken leg observes his neighbors. He suspects one of them of murder.

critics.quote: Four Oscar nominations including Best Director.

items.title: North by Northwest
items.whyPriority: His most influential — every action thriller since owes it. Cary Grant on the run, the crop-duster, Mount Rushmore.
items.pitch: An advertising executive is mistaken for a spy and pursued across America.

critics.quote: Three Oscar nominations. AFI Top 100.

items.title: Psycho
items.whyPriority: Technically just outside our 1960 cutoff but listed for completeness. The shower scene. The Bates Motel.
items.pitch: A secretary embezzles money and stops at a roadside motel.

critics.quote: Four Oscar nominations including Best Director. Cultural earthquake.

items.title: Notorious
items.whyPriority: His best espionage film. Bergman and Grant. Post-WWII Brazilian Nazis. The kissing-while-on-the-phone scene worked around the Code.
items.pitch: An American agent recruits a German spy's daughter to infiltrate a Nazi enclave in Rio.

critics.quote: Two Oscar nominations. Often cited among Hitchcock's masterpieces.

items.title: Strangers on a Train
items.pitch: Two men meet on a train. One proposes they swap murders. The first man laughs it off. The second is serious.

critics.quote: Routinely cited among Hitchcock's best.

items.title: Rope
items.pitch: Two friends murder a third and host a dinner party with the body in the trunk. Famously appears to be one continuous shot.

items.title: Shadow of a Doubt
items.pitch: Hitchcock's personal favorite. A young woman's beloved uncle visits. He may be a serial killer.

critics.quote: Hitchcock cited it as his personal favorite.

items.title: Dial M for Murder
items.pitch: Husband plans to kill his wife. The plan goes wrong. Originally shot in 3D.

items.title: The 39 Steps
items.pitch: His British-period masterpiece. The model for North by Northwest. An innocent man wrongly accused, on the run.

sections.name: II. Billy Wilder
sections.desc: Vienna-born American. Tonal range from noir to romantic comedy.

items.title: Sunset Boulevard
items.whyPriority: His masterpiece. A struggling screenwriter narrates his own death. Gloria Swanson as the silent-era star refusing to fade.
items.pitch: An ambitious screenwriter washes up at a faded silent-era star's mansion. He becomes her companion.

critics.quote: Won 3 Oscars. Eleven nominations.

items.title: Some Like It Hot
items.whyPriority: Routinely named the greatest American comedy. Lemmon and Curtis as musicians who dress as women to escape the mob. Marilyn Monroe.
items.pitch: Two Chicago musicians witness a mob hit and join an all-female band heading to Florida.

critics.quote: AFI's #1 American Comedy. Six Oscar nominations.

items.title: The Apartment
items.whyPriority: Listed despite slight cutoff overlap. Won Best Picture. Lemmon's lonely insurance clerk lends his apartment to executives. Romantic, sad, sharp.
items.pitch: A New York insurance clerk lets executives use his apartment for affairs. He falls for the elevator operator.

critics.quote: Won 5 Oscars including Best Picture, Best Director.

items.title: Double Indemnity
items.pitch: Cross-listed from crime.

items.title: Witness for the Prosecution
items.pitch: Agatha Christie courtroom drama. Charles Laughton as the barrister. Tyrone Power, Marlene Dietrich. Twist ending.

critics.quote: Six Oscar nominations.

items.title: Stalag 17
items.pitch: WWII POW camp. Holden won Best Actor. A mole in the barracks. Wilder treats it as comedy and tragedy at once.

critics.quote: Won Best Actor (Holden).

items.title: Ace in the Hole
items.pitch: Kirk Douglas as a corrupt journalist who delays a man's rescue from a cave-in to maximize his story. Wilder at his most cynical.

critics.quote: Failed on release; reputation has grown enormously. Now considered one of his masterpieces.

sections.name: III. John Ford
sections.desc: Westerns + The Grapes of Wrath. American mythmaker.

items.title: The Searchers
items.whyPriority: Often named the greatest American Western. Wayne's most morally complex performance. Monument Valley as American Eden.
items.pitch: A Civil War veteran searches for years for his niece, kidnapped by Comanches. His search becomes obsession.

critics.quote: Sight & Sound canonical. AFI Top 25.

items.title: Stagecoach
items.whyPriority: Made Wayne a star. Established the Western archetypes for the next 70 years.
items.pitch: A stagecoach passes through Apache territory. The passengers carry their secrets.

critics.quote: Won 2 Oscars including Best Supporting Actor (Mitchell).

items.title: The Grapes of Wrath
items.pitch: Cross-listed from drama.

items.title: The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance
items.whyPriority: Late Ford masterpiece. Wayne and Stewart. The end of the Western myth. 'Print the legend.'
items.pitch: A senator returns to a small Western town for a funeral and remembers his beginning there.

critics.quote: Routinely cited as one of the greatest Westerns. AFI canonical.

items.title: How Green Was My Valley
items.pitch: Welsh mining village across decades. Famously beat Citizen Kane for Best Picture in 1942.

critics.quote: Won 5 Oscars including Best Picture and Best Director.

items.title: My Darling Clementine
items.pitch: Wyatt Earp at the OK Corral. Henry Fonda. The most lyrical Earp film.

sections.name: IV. Orson Welles
sections.desc: American. Career interrupted constantly; masterpieces nonetheless.

items.title: Citizen Kane
items.whyPriority: Welles's debut at age 25. The film that taught modern cinema to itself. Was the consensus greatest film for 50 years.
items.pitch: A reporter investigates the meaning of a media tycoon's last word: 'Rosebud.'

critics.quote: Topped the Greatest Films poll for 50 consecutive years before being displaced by Vertigo in 2012.

items.title: Touch of Evil
items.pitch: Cross-listed from crime. Watch the 1998 reconstruction edit.

items.title: The Magnificent Ambersons
items.whyPriority: His sophomore film. The studio destroyed 40 minutes and reshot the ending. Even mutilated, it's a masterpiece.
items.pitch: An aristocratic Midwestern family declines as the modern industrial age rises around them.

critics.quote: Four Oscar nominations including Best Picture.

items.title: Chimes at Midnight
items.pitch: Cross-listed from drama / Shakespeare.

items.title: F for Fake
items.pitch: Welles's late-period essay-film about art forgers. Non-fiction that may not be non-fiction.

items.title: The Trial
items.pitch: Welles adapting Kafka. Anthony Perkins as Josef K. Visually astonishing.

critics.quote: Welles considered it his best film. Critical reputation has grown.

items.title: The Rules of the Game
items.pitch: Cross-listed from foreign.

items.title: Grand Illusion
items.whyPriority: WWI POW film. First foreign-language Best Picture nominee. Renoir's most accessible great film.
items.pitch: French officers in a German POW camp during WWI. Class divisions across enemy lines.

critics.quote: First foreign-language Best Picture nominee.

items.title: Bicycle Thieves
items.pitch: Cross-listed from foreign.

items.title: Ikiru
items.pitch: Cross-listed from auteur (Kurosawa).

items.title: Tokyo Story
items.pitch: Cross-listed from foreign.

items.title: The Battleship Potemkin
items.whyPriority: The Odessa Steps sequence is the most analyzed in film history. Soviet propaganda as artistic milestone.
items.pitch: 1905 mutiny aboard a Russian battleship.

items.title: M
items.pitch: Lang's first sound film. Peter Lorre as a child murderer hunted by both police and criminals. The original serial-killer film.

items.title: Metropolis
items.pitch: Foundational German Expressionist sci-fi. Watch the 2010 restored version (with rediscovered footage).

items.title: The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari
items.pitch: German Expressionism's defining text. Painted-set unreality. Influence on horror, noir, Lynch.

items.title: The Passion of Joan of Arc
items.pitch: Silent. Maria Falconetti's face is one of the great close-up performances ever filmed.

critics.quote: Routinely listed among the greatest silent films and greatest films of all time.

sections.name: VI. Pre-Code (1929-1934)
sections.desc: Brief era of pre-censorship Hollywood freedom. Sex, violence, irony unconstrained.

items.title: Trouble in Paradise
items.whyPriority: The peak of pre-Code sophistication. Two thieves try to con a Parisian heiress. Witty, sexy, never bested.
items.pitch: Two con artists fall in love. They target a wealthy widow. Things get complicated when one of them falls for her.

critics.quote: Routinely cited as the apex of the 'Lubitsch touch' and the pre-Code era.

items.title: Baby Face
items.whyPriority: Most explicit pre-Code Stanwyck. A young woman uses sex to climb the corporate ladder. The film that helped trigger Code enforcement.
items.pitch: Barbara Stanwyck plays a small-town woman who methodically seduces her way up a New York bank's hierarchy.

critics.quote: Censored on release; restored in 2004. Now widely considered a pre-Code masterpiece.

items.title: I Am a Fugitive from a Chain Gang
items.pitch: Paul Muni as a wrongly convicted Depression-era veteran. The film that helped reform Southern chain-gang systems.

critics.quote: Three Oscar nominations.

items.title: Heroes for Sale
items.pitch: WWI veteran's downward spiral through unemployment, addiction, Depression. Pre-Code social-issue cinema at its bleakest.

items.title: Footlight Parade
items.pitch: Busby Berkeley pre-Code musical. Cagney + Joan Blondell. The 'By a Waterfall' production number is the kind of choreography only the pre-Code era allowed.

items.title: Scarface
items.pitch: Original gangster epic. Loosely based on Al Capone. Influence on every gangster film since (including the De Palma remake).

items.title: Red Dust
items.pitch: Gable + Jean Harlow on an Indochinese rubber plantation. The kind of romantic-comedy plotting that became impossible after 1934.
